subject: Anatomy
front: In these notes, which cranial-nerve functional column carries the special senses?
back: SSA – special somatic afferent (olfaction, vision, hearing).

subject: Anatomy
front: Which functional column supplies the muscles derived from the pharyngeal arches (branchial motor)?
back: SVE – special visceral efferent.

subject: Anatomy
front: Which functional column carries taste?
back: SVA – special visceral afferent (written 'SVA-ad').

subject: Anatomy
front: Which functional column carries general sensation?
back: GSA – general somatic afferent.

subject: Anatomy
front: Which functional column gives parasympathetic supply to glands?
back: GVE – general visceral efferent.

subject: Anatomy
front: Which functional column carries visceral (afferent) sensation?
back: GVA – general visceral afferent.

subject: Anatomy
front: Which functional column supplies the somatic (non-branchial) muscles?
back: GSE – general somatic efferent.

subject: Anatomy
front: What mnemonic is used for the seven cranial-nerve functional columns?
back: "SAVE VA SAVE VASE" (the seven columns being SSA, SVE, SVA, GSA, GVE, GVA, GSE).

subject: Anatomy
front: Which cranial nerves carry the special senses (SSA), and which sense does each mediate?
back: CN I – olfaction; CN II – vision; CN VIII – audition/hearing.

subject: Anatomy
front: Which nucleus/cranial nerve supplies the muscles of each pharyngeal arch (SVE column)?
back: 1st arch – motor nucleus of CN V; 2nd arch – motor nucleus of CN VII; 3rd arch – CN IX (nucleus ambiguus); 4th & 6th arch – CN X (nucleus ambiguus).

subject: Anatomy
front: Which cranial nerves carry taste (SVA), and from which region?
back: CN VII – anterior 2/3 of tongue; CN IX – posterior 1/3 of tongue; CN X – posterior-most tongue, uvula and palate. (CN V carries no taste.)

subject: Anatomy
front: Which cranial nerves provide general sensation (GSA), and to where?
back: CN V – face; CN VII – minor supply to the ear; CN IX – Jacobson's nerve; CN X – Arnold's nerve.

subject: Anatomy
front: Which nuclei/cranial nerves give parasympathetic gland supply (GVE column)?
back: CN III – Edinger-Westphal nucleus; CN VII – superior salivatory & lacrimatory nucleus; CN IX – inferior salivatory nucleus; CN X – dorsal vagal nucleus.

subject: Anatomy
front: Which cranial nerves form the visceral afferent (GVA) column?
back: CN VII, CN IX and CN X — all terminating in the nucleus tractus solitarius (NTS).

subject: Anatomy
front: Which cranial nerves form the somatic motor (GSE) column, and what do they supply?
back: CN III, IV, VI – extraocular muscles (EOM); CN XI – sternocleidomastoid & trapezius; CN XII – tongue.

subject: Anatomy
front: All taste (SVA) and visceral afferent (GVA) fibres end in which nucleus?
back: Nucleus tractus solitarius (NTS / nucleus solitarius).

subject: Anatomy
front: The nucleus tractus solitarius (NTS) receives which three kinds of input?
back: Taste, baroreceptor input, and gut distension.

subject: Anatomy
front: List the cranial-nerve nuclear columns in the brainstem from medial to lateral.
back: GSE – SVE – GVE – GVA – SVA – GSA – SSA.

subject: Anatomy
front: How do the alar and basal plates map onto sensory vs motor cranial nerve nuclei?
back: Alar plate (dorsal/lateral) → sensory nuclei; basal plate (ventral) → motor nuclei. Mnemonic 'DAS': Dorsal = lateral = Alar = Sensory.

subject: Anatomy
front: Match these medullary nuclei to their functional columns: dorsal vagal nucleus, nucleus ambiguus, hypoglossal nucleus, nucleus of solitary tract, spinal trigeminal nucleus, vestibulocochlear nucleus.
back: Dorsal vagal nucleus = GVE (parasympathetic); nucleus ambiguus = SVE (branchial motor); hypoglossal nucleus = GSE; nucleus of solitary tract = GVA/SVA (visceral afferent incl. taste); spinal trigeminal nucleus = GSA; vestibulocochlear nucleus = SSA.

subject: Anatomy
front: What are the three sensory nuclei of the trigeminal (GSA) system, with their locations and modalities?
back: Mesencephalic nucleus (midbrain) – proprioception (jaw jerk, V3), a CNS-only pseudounipolar neuron; principal/chief sensory nucleus (pons) – touch & pressure; spinal nucleus (medulla) – pain & temperature.

subject: Anatomy
front: Trace the CN III parasympathetic pathway (nucleus → ganglion → postganglionic nerve → target).
back: Edinger-Westphal nucleus → ciliary ganglion → nasociliary/short ciliary nerves → ciliary muscle (and sphincter pupillae); action = miosis and accommodation.

subject: Anatomy
front: The ciliary muscle develops from which embryonic tissue?
back: Neural crest cells (NCC derivative).

subject: Anatomy
front: Trace the CN VII parasympathetic pathway to the lacrimal gland.
back: Superior salivatory/lacrimatory nucleus → geniculate ganglion → greater superficial petrosal nerve (GSPN) → pterygopalatine ganglion → maxillary nerve (V2), zygomatic branch → lacrimal gland.

subject: Anatomy
front: Trace the CN VII parasympathetic pathway to the submandibular and sublingual glands.
back: Superior salivatory nucleus → chorda tympani → submandibular ganglion → lingual nerve (V3) → submandibular and sublingual glands.

subject: Anatomy
front: Trace the CN IX parasympathetic pathway to the parotid gland.
back: Inferior salivatory nucleus → otic ganglion → auriculotemporal nerve → parotid gland.

subject: Anatomy
front: Aberrant regeneration in the auriculotemporal (parotid) parasympathetic pathway causes which syndrome?
back: Frey's syndrome (gustatory sweating).

subject: Anatomy
front: Trace the CN X parasympathetic pathway.
back: Dorsal motor nucleus of vagus → vagal nerve → heart, lungs and upper GI (no peripheral ganglion listed).

subject: Anatomy
front: Stimulation of Arnold's nerve (auricular branch of vagus) can cause what?
back: Syncope (Arnold's nerve → syncope).

subject: Anatomy
front: What does excessive vagal parasympathetic discharge cause, per the notes?
back: Immersion syndrome / vasovagal shock, and death due to vagal parasympathetic discharge.

subject: Anatomy
front: Accommodation reflex – afferent and efferent limbs?
back: Afferent CN II; efferent CN III.

subject: Anatomy
front: Pupillary (light) reflex – afferent and efferent limbs?
back: Afferent CN II; efferent CN III.

subject: Anatomy
front: Corneal reflex – afferent and efferent limbs?
back: Afferent V1 (ophthalmic); efferent bilateral CN VII to orbicularis oculi (via its temporal & zygomatic branches).

subject: Anatomy
front: Lacrimation reflex – afferent and efferent limbs?
back: Afferent V1; efferent CN VII (mediates reflex tears, NOT emotional tears).

subject: Anatomy
front: Cough reflex – afferent and efferent limbs?
back: Afferent CN X; efferent CN X.

subject: Anatomy
front: Gag reflex – afferent and efferent limbs?
back: Afferent CN IX; efferent CN X.

subject: Anatomy
front: Jaw jerk reflex – afferent and efferent limbs?
back: Afferent V3 (from muscle spindles); efferent V3.

subject: Anatomy
front: What passes through the cribriform plate, and what is unique about this nerve's central connection?
back: CN I (olfactory). It is the only sensory pathway that does NOT relay in the thalamus — it projects directly to the primary olfactory (piriform) cortex/amygdala.

subject: Anatomy
front: What passes through the optic canal, and which bone forms it?
back: CN II (optic nerve) and the ophthalmic artery; the canal is in the lesser wing of the sphenoid (LWS).

subject: Anatomy
front: What passes through the superior orbital fissure (SOF), and between which bones does it lie?
back: CN III, CN IV, CN VI and V1 (ophthalmic); it lies between the greater and lesser wings of the sphenoid (GWS–LWS).

subject: Anatomy
front: What passes through the foramen rotundum?
back: V2 (maxillary nerve).

subject: Anatomy
front: What passes through the foramen ovale (mnemonic 'MALE')?
back: MALE – Mandibular nerve (V3), Accessory meningeal artery, Lesser petrosal nerve, Emissary vein.

subject: Anatomy
front: What passes through the foramen spinosum?
back: Middle meningeal artery (MMA) and the nervus spinosus (recurrent meningeal branch of V3).

subject: Anatomy
front: What is the ICA's relationship to the foramen lacerum, and which bones meet there?
back: The internal carotid artery passes OVER (across the top of) the lacerum, not through it. The lacerum sits at the junction of the sphenoid, occipital and petrous (temporal) bones; the Vidian nerve also runs here.

subject: Anatomy
front: All three trigeminal divisions leave the skull by their own exit — name each exit.
back: V1 (ophthalmic) → SOF; V2 (maxillary) → foramen rotundum; V3 (mandibular) → foramen ovale.

subject: Anatomy
front: What passes through the internal acoustic meatus (IAM)?
back: CN VII, CN VIII, and the labyrinthine artery (a branch of AICA).

subject: Anatomy
front: How is the fundus of the internal acoustic meatus divided, and what lies in each quadrant?
back: The transverse (falciform) crest separates superior from inferior halves; Bill's bar (vertical crest) splits the superior half. Superior-anterior = facial nerve (VII); superior-posterior = superior vestibular nerve; inferior-anterior = cochlear nerve; inferior-posterior = inferior vestibular nerve.

subject: Anatomy
front: What passes through the jugular foramen?
back: CN IX, X and XI, the internal jugular vein, and the inferior petrosal & sigmoid sinuses.

subject: Anatomy
front: How is the jugular foramen divided into pars nervosa and pars vascularis, and what does each transmit?
back: Pars nervosa – CN IX (glossopharyngeal, in its own dural sheath) + inferior petrosal sinus; pars vascularis – internal jugular vein + CN X and CN XI.

subject: Anatomy
front: What passes through the hypoglossal canal?
back: CN XII (hypoglossal nerve).

subject: Anatomy
front: What passes through the foramen magnum?
back: The medulla oblongata (NOT the spinal cord), the vertebral arteries, the anterior spinal artery, and the spinal part of CN XI (spinal accessory).

subject: Anatomy
front: Which craniovertebral-junction ligaments are related to the foramen magnum region?
back: Apical ligament of the dens, cruciform ligament, and membrana tectoria.

subject: Anatomy
front: What tumour/sign is annotated at the jugular foramen in the notes?
back: Glomus jugulare (glomus tumour); the slide also marks a 'PHELP sign' and 'Griesinger sign' near this region [exact wording partly unclear in the source photo].

subject: Anatomy
front: Which structures pass ABOVE the common tendinous ring (annulus of Zinn) in the SOF?
back: Lacrimal, Frontal and Trochlear nerves (mnemonic 'LFT' – they pass above), plus the superior ophthalmic vein.

subject: Anatomy
front: Which structures pass THROUGH the annulus of Zinn (within the tendinous ring)?
back: Both divisions of the oculomotor nerve (III), the nasociliary nerve (V1), and the abducens nerve (VI).

subject: Anatomy
front: Which four bones meet at the pterion, and why is it clinically important?
back: Frontal, Parietal, Temporal (squamous) and Sphenoid (greater wing). It is the thinnest part of the skull; the anterior division of the middle meningeal artery lies deep to it, so a fracture can cause an extradural (epidural) haematoma.

subject: Anatomy
front: What structures lie deep to the pterion?
back: Anterior division of the middle meningeal artery, lesser wing of sphenoid, stem of the lateral sulcus, the Sylvian fissure/MCA, insular cortex and Broca's area.

subject: Anatomy
front: What are the two components of the Vidian nerve (nerve of the pterygoid canal)?
back: Greater superficial petrosal nerve (GSPN – parasympathetic, carries lacrimation) plus the deep petrosal nerve (DPN – sympathetic from the plexus around the ICA).

subject: Anatomy
front: Vidian neurectomy is performed for which condition?
back: Vasomotor rhinitis.

subject: Anatomy
front: What is the cranial-nerve 'Rule of 17'?
back: Pairs of cranial nerves whose numbers add to 17 behave together: CN X(10)+CN VII(7) [uvula, nasolabial fold] deviate to the CONTRALATERAL side; CN V(5)+CN XII(12) [jaw, tongue] deviate to the IPSILATERAL side.

subject: Anatomy
front: In the Rule of 17, which structures does the 10+7 pair govern, and in which direction do they deviate in a lesion?
back: Uvula (CN X) and nasolabial fold/angle of mouth (CN VII) — deviate to the contralateral side.

subject: Anatomy
front: In the Rule of 17, which structures does the 5+12 pair govern, and in which direction do they deviate in a lesion?
back: Jaw (CN V) and tongue (CN XII) — deviate to the ipsilateral (same) side.

subject: Anatomy
front: Which cranial nerve has a dorsal origin, decussates internally, has the longest intracranial course, and is the thinnest/smallest?
back: CN IV (trochlear nerve) — all four features.

subject: Anatomy
front: Which cranial nerve has the longest intradural course, and in which canal?
back: CN VI (abducens) — in Dorello's canal.

subject: Anatomy
front: Which cranial nerve has the longest intraosseous course, and in which canal?
back: CN VII (facial) — in the facial (Fallopian) canal.

subject: Anatomy
front: Name the parts of the corpus callosum from anterior to posterior (mnemonic RGBS).
back: Rostrum, Genu, Body, Splenium.

subject: Anatomy
front: What two structures make up the lentiform (lenticular) nucleus?
back: Putamen + globus pallidus.

subject: Anatomy
front: What two structures make up the striatum?
back: Caudate nucleus + putamen.

subject: Anatomy
front: Going lateral to medial from the insula on the coronal section, list the structures.
back: Insular cortex → extreme capsule → claustrum → external capsule → putamen → globus pallidus (with the internal capsule medial to the lentiform nucleus).

subject: Anatomy
front: What is the insular ribbon sign and what does it indicate?
back: In an MCA stroke (MCA = most commonly affected artery), the insular cortex is the first region to become hypodense; loss of the normal insular grey-white 'ribbon' is an early CT sign.

subject: Anatomy
front: The lateral sulcus (Sylvian fissure) contains which artery?
back: The middle cerebral artery (MCA).

subject: Anatomy
front: Trace the Papez circuit.
back: Hippocampus → fornix → mammillary body → (mammillothalamic tract) → anterior thalamic nucleus → cingulate gyrus → entorhinal cortex → subiculum → back to hippocampus (part of the limbic system).

subject: Anatomy
front: The fornix connects which two structures?
back: Hippocampus and mammillary body.

subject: Anatomy
front: What forms the ANTERIOR wall of the third ventricle?
back: Anterior commissure, lamina terminalis, and the anterior column of the fornix.

subject: Anatomy
front: What forms the ROOF of the third ventricle?
back: Fornix, choroid plexus and ependyma (tela choroidea).

subject: Anatomy
front: What forms the POSTERIOR wall of the third ventricle?
back: Pineal gland, habenular commissure, posterior commissure and the beginning of the cerebral aqueduct (with velum interpositum + internal cerebral veins).

subject: Anatomy
front: What forms the FLOOR of the third ventricle?
back: Optic chiasma, infundibulum, tuber cinereum, mammillary bodies, posterior perforated substance and tegmentum of the midbrain.

subject: Anatomy
front: What forms the LATERAL wall of the third ventricle?
back: Thalamus and hypothalamus, separated by the hypothalamic sulcus.

subject: Anatomy
front: What are the special features of the lamina terminalis?
back: It lacks a blood-brain barrier (a circumventricular organ), senses thirst & osmolarity, and marks where the cranial (anterior) neuropore closes.

subject: Anatomy
front: In the body of the lateral ventricle, what forms the roof, floor and medial wall?
back: Roof = trunk of corpus callosum; floor = caudate nucleus, stria terminalis, thalamostriate vein and thalamus; medial wall = septum pellucidum.

subject: Anatomy
front: What forms the bulb and the calcar avis in the posterior horn of the lateral ventricle?
back: The bulb is formed by fibres of the forceps major; the calcar avis is an elevation produced by the calcarine sulcus (tapetum and optic radiation lie in the wall).

subject: Anatomy
front: What is the tectal plate (corpora quadrigemina) made of, and the function of each colliculus?
back: The superior and inferior colliculi. Superior colliculus – visual reflexes; inferior colliculus – auditory relay.

subject: Anatomy
front: How is the facial colliculus formed?
back: By fibres of the facial nerve (CN VII) looping (hooking) around the abducens (CN VI) nucleus in the floor of the 4th ventricle.

subject: Anatomy
front: What does the sulcus limitans separate in the floor of the 4th ventricle?
back: Medial motor nuclei from the lateral sensory nuclei.

subject: Anatomy
front: Name the three cerebellar peduncles.
back: Superior (SCP), middle (MCP) and inferior (ICP) cerebellar peduncles.

subject: Anatomy
front: What two 'trigones' are seen in the floor of the 4th ventricle?
back: The hypoglossal trigone and the vagal trigone (with the stria medullaris).

subject: Anatomy
front: What passes through the GENU of the internal capsule?
back: The corticobulbar tract.

subject: Anatomy
front: What does the ANTERIOR limb of the internal capsule contain, and what is its blood supply?
back: Anterior thalamic radiation and frontopontine (corticofugal) fibres; supplied by the ACA (recurrent artery of Heubner).

subject: Anatomy
front: What does the POSTERIOR limb of the internal capsule contain, and its blood supply?
back: Corticospinal tract, superior thalamic radiation (somatosensory) and corticopontine fibres; supplied by the MCA (lenticulostriate / Charcot's artery) + anterior choroidal artery.

subject: Anatomy
front: What is the somatotopic order of fibres in the posterior limb of the internal capsule?
back: Arm – Trunk – Leg (A-T-L), anterior to posterior.

subject: Anatomy
front: What does the SUBLENTIFORM part of the internal capsule carry, and its supply?
back: The auditory radiation (inferior thalamic peduncle, from the medial geniculate nucleus); supplied by PCA + anterior choroidal artery.

subject: Anatomy
front: What does the RETROLENTIFORM part of the internal capsule carry, and its supply?
back: The optic radiation (posterior thalamic peduncle, from the lateral geniculate nucleus); supplied by PCA + anterior choroidal artery.

subject: Anatomy
front: Which geniculate nucleus is auditory and which is visual?
back: Medial geniculate nucleus – auditory; lateral geniculate nucleus – visual.

subject: Anatomy
front: What are commissural fibres, and give examples?
back: Fibres connecting the right and left cerebral hemispheres — e.g. corpus callosum, anterior commissure, posterior commissure, habenular commissure.

subject: Anatomy
front: What are association fibres, and give an example?
back: Fibres connecting two areas within the same hemisphere (side to side) — e.g. the arcuate fasciculus, which connects Broca's and Wernicke's areas.

subject: Anatomy
front: What are projection fibres, and give examples?
back: Fibres connecting the cortex to lower centres — e.g. corticospinal tract, corticobulbar tract, and the internal capsule.

subject: Anatomy
front: The fornix contains which fibre types, and in what proportion?
back: All three types, in the order association > commissural > projection.

subject: Anatomy
front: What is the TASTE supply of the tongue by region?
back: Anterior 2/3 – CN VII via chorda tympani; posterior 1/3 (incl. vallate papillae) – CN IX; posterior-most tongue/epiglottic region – CN X via the internal laryngeal nerve (ILN).

subject: Anatomy
front: What is the GENERAL SENSORY supply of the tongue by region?
back: Anterior 2/3 – lingual nerve (CN V3); posterior 1/3 – CN IX; posterior-most/epiglottic region – CN X (internal laryngeal nerve).

subject: Anatomy
front: Taste and sensation from the vallate (circumvallate) papillae are carried by which nerve, despite them lying anterior to the sulcus terminalis?
back: CN IX (glossopharyngeal).

subject: Anatomy
front: Which tongue papillae have taste buds and which do not?
back: Fungiform, circumvallate (vallate) and foliate papillae have taste buds; filiform papillae have NO taste buds.

subject: Anatomy
front: Which nerve supplies all the tongue muscles, and what is the exception?
back: All tongue muscles are supplied by CN XII (hypoglossal) except palatoglossus, which is supplied by CN X via the pharyngeal plexus.

subject: Anatomy
front: From which embryonic source do the tongue muscles develop?
back: Occipital myotomes.

subject: Anatomy
front: Which tongue muscle is the 'safety muscle,' and what is its action?
back: Genioglossus – the protruder of the tongue. Loss of its tone (e.g. in an unconscious patient) lets the tongue fall back, so jaw-thrust/chin-lift is used.

subject: Anatomy
front: Which tongue muscle is the depressor?
back: Hyoglossus.

subject: Anatomy
front: What is the action of styloglossus?
back: Elevation and retraction of the tongue.

subject: Anatomy
front: Name the extrinsic tongue muscles and their key actions.
back: Genioglossus – protruder (safety muscle); hyoglossus – depressor; styloglossus – elevates + retracts; palatoglossus – elevates root (CN X). Geniohyoid (shown alongside) elevates the hyoid.

subject: Anatomy
front: What are the arterial supply and venous drainage of the tongue?
back: Lingual artery (from the external carotid, ECA); lingual vein drains to the internal jugular vein (IJV).

subject: Anatomy
front: In a CN XII lesion, which way does the tongue deviate on protrusion — UMN vs LMN?
back: UMN (supranuclear) lesion → tongue deviates to the CONTRALATERAL side; LMN (nuclear/infranuclear) lesion → genioglossus paralysed on the lesion side, tongue deviates to the IPSILATERAL (same) side.

subject: Anatomy
front: Name the four muscles of mastication.
back: Temporalis, masseter, lateral pterygoid and medial pterygoid.

subject: Anatomy
front: Which nerve supplies the muscles of mastication, and what is special about the medial pterygoid?
back: The mandibular nerve (V3) — via its anterior division, except the medial pterygoid, whose nerve arises from the MAIN TRUNK of V3.

subject: Anatomy
front: Which muscle of mastication is NOT an elevator of the mandible, and what does it do?
back: Lateral pterygoid — it lowers/depresses (opens) the jaw; all the others are elevators.

subject: Anatomy
front: Which muscle of mastication is a retractor rather than a protractor?
back: Temporalis — it retracts the mandible; the others protract (protrude) it.

subject: Anatomy
front: Which muscle of mastication is attached to the articular disc of the TMJ?
back: Lateral pterygoid.

subject: Anatomy
front: What is the accessory muscle of mastication, and its nerve supply?
back: Buccinator — supplied by CN VII (facial nerve).

subject: Anatomy
front: Which ligaments stabilise the cranio-vertebral junction?
back: Tectorial membrane (upward continuation of the PLL), cruciate ligament, apical ligament of the dens, alar ligaments, and the anterior & posterior atlanto-occipital membranes (with ALL/PLL and ligamentum flavum).

subject: Anatomy
front: What forms the cruciate ligament, and which structure is stressed in judicial (hangman's) hanging?
back: Cruciate ligament = transverse ligament of the atlas + longitudinal (vertical) bands; the transverse ligament of the dens is the key stabiliser disrupted at the cranio-vertebral junction in judicial hanging.

subject: Anatomy
front: What is the function of the alar ligaments?
back: They connect the dens to the occipital condyles and limit rotation of the head.

subject: Anatomy
front: Neural crest derivatives — expand the mnemonic 'MOTEL PASSES'.
back: M-Melanocytes; O-Odontoblasts (dentin); T-Tracheal cartilage; E-Enterochromaffin cells & enteric plexus; L-Leptomeninges; P-PNS (all ganglia), Parafollicular C cells, Pharyngeal arch cartilage; A-Adrenal medulla; S-Schwann cells; S-Spiral (aorticopulmonary) septum; E-Endocardial cushions (→ AV & semilunar valves); S-Skull cartilage & dermis of head/neck.

subject: Anatomy
front: Which cardiac structures are neural-crest derived?
back: The spiral (aorticopulmonary) septum and the endocardial cushions (which form the AV and semilunar valves).

subject: Anatomy
front: Adrenal cortex vs adrenal medulla — germ-layer origin?
back: Adrenal cortex – intermediate mesoderm; adrenal medulla – neural crest.

subject: Anatomy
front: Dura vs leptomeninges — origin?
back: Dura (outer) – paraxial mesoderm; leptomeninges/pia-arachnoid (inner) – neural crest. ('Bahar wale = meso, andar wala = NC.')

subject: Anatomy
front: Enamel vs dentin — origin?
back: Enamel/ameloblasts – surface ectoderm; dentin/odontoblasts – neural crest.

subject: Anatomy
front: What is the germ-layer origin of the parafollicular (C) cells of the thyroid?
back: Neural crest.

subject: Anatomy
front: What are the derivatives of the notochord?
back: Nucleus pulposus, tectorial membrane, and the apical ligament of the dens.

subject: Anatomy
front: The tectorial membrane is the superior continuation of which ligament?
back: The posterior longitudinal ligament (PLL).

subject: Anatomy
front: What does the intermediate mesoderm give rise to?
back: The genitourinary (GU) system, adrenal cortex, and gonads.

subject: Anatomy
front: What does the paraxial mesoderm (somites) give rise to?
back: Dura, axial skeleton, dermis of the back, skeletal muscles, and tongue/EOM muscles.

subject: Anatomy
front: What does the SOMATOPLEURIC layer of lateral plate mesoderm give rise to?
back: Dermis of the anterior torso & limbs, and the appendicular skeleton.

subject: Anatomy
front: What does the SPLANCHNOPLEURIC layer of lateral plate mesoderm give rise to?
back: Cardiac muscle, smooth muscle, the RES (incl. spleen), and serosa/endothelium.

subject: Anatomy
front: List the surface-ectoderm derivatives.
back: Anterior pituitary (Rathke's pouch); eye ('LEL' – lens, epithelium of conjunctiva & cornea, lacrimal & tarsal glands); external auditory canal & tympanic membrane (outer); glands ('PSM' – parotid, sweat, mammary); anal canal below the pectinate line; epidermis; olfactory epithelium; enamel.

subject: Anatomy
front: The anterior pituitary develops from what, and what tumour arises from its remnant?
back: Rathke's pouch (surface/oral ectoderm); a craniopharyngioma.

subject: Anatomy
front: Which eye structures are surface-ectoderm derived (mnemonic 'LEL')?
back: Lens; Epithelium of conjunctiva & cornea; Lacrimal & tarsal glands.

subject: Anatomy
front: Which three glands are surface-ectoderm derived (mnemonic 'PSM')?
back: Parotid, sweat and mammary glands.

subject: Anatomy
front: List the neuroectoderm derivatives.
back: Brain, spinal cord, neurons and glial cells (EXCEPT microglia); pineal gland; posterior pituitary; and eye – dilator & sphincter pupillae, retina, iris epithelium, ciliary epithelium and optic nerve.

subject: Anatomy
front: Which CNS cell type is NOT neuroectodermal, and what is its origin?
back: Microglia — they are mesodermal (part of the RES).

subject: Anatomy
front: What are the main endoderm derivatives, and the key exception?
back: The whole GI tract/abdomen (epithelium) and the urogenital sinus; EXCEPT the spleen, which is mesodermal.

subject: Anatomy
front: Give the germ-layer origin of the eye muscles: dilator & sphincter pupillae, ciliaris, and EOM.
back: Dilator & sphincter pupillae – neuroectoderm; ciliaris (ciliary muscle) – neural crest; extraocular muscles (EOM) – mesoderm (paraxial).

subject: Anatomy
front: Which structure(s) are derived from all three germ layers?
back: The tympanic membrane (derived from ectoderm, mesoderm and endoderm).

subject: Anatomy
front: When does the cranial (anterior) neuropore close, and what results from failure?
back: Day 25; failure causes anencephaly.

subject: Anatomy
front: When does the caudal (posterior) neuropore close, and what results from failure?
back: Day 27/28; failure causes neural tube defects (spina bifida / spinal NTDs).

subject: Anatomy
front: Name the stages of fetal lung development in order, and when respiration becomes possible.
back: Embryonic → pseudoglandular → canalicular → saccular → alveolar; respiration becomes possible in the canalicular stage (~24 weeks).

subject: Anatomy
front: The mesonephric duct is also known as?
back: The Wolffian duct.

subject: Anatomy
front: What does the caudal end of the mesonephric duct (the ureteric bud) form?
back: Collecting duct, calyces, renal pelvis, ureter, and the trigone of the bladder.

subject: Anatomy
front: In males, what does the mesonephric duct form (mnemonic 'SEED')?
back: Seminal vesicle, Epididymis, Ejaculatory duct, and Ductus (vas) deferens.

subject: Anatomy
front: In females, what are the vestigial mesonephric-duct remnants (mnemonic 'PEG'), and where do they lie?
back: Paraoophoron, Epoophoron and Gartner's duct — lying in the broad ligament.

subject: Anatomy
front: A Gartner's duct cyst (mesonephric remnant) is found where?
back: In the anterior vaginal wall.

subject: Anatomy
front: The paramesonephric duct is also known as?
back: The Müllerian duct.

subject: Anatomy
front: In females, what does the paramesonephric (Müllerian) duct form?
back: The fallopian tubes, uterus, cervix and upper 2/3 of the vagina.

subject: Anatomy
front: What forms the lower 1/3 of the vagina (not the Müllerian duct)?
back: The urogenital sinus (UGS).

subject: Anatomy
front: Is the ovary a Müllerian derivative?
back: No — the ovary develops from the genital ridge/gonad, not the paramesonephric duct.

subject: Anatomy
front: In males, what are the paramesonephric (Müllerian) remnants (mnemonic 'PAT')?
back: Prostatic utricle (masculine vagina) and the appendix of testis (Hydatid of Morgagni).

subject: Anatomy
front: Genital tubercle — male and female derivatives?
back: Glans penis (male); clitoris (female).

subject: Anatomy
front: Genital (urethral) fold — male and female derivatives?
back: Ventral shaft of penis (male); labia minora (female).

subject: Anatomy
front: Genital (labioscrotal) swelling — male and female derivatives?
back: Scrotum (male); labia majora (female).

subject: Anatomy
front: Genital ridge (gonad) — male and female derivatives?
back: Testes (male); ovary (female).

subject: Anatomy
front: The kidney (up to the DCT) develops from what?
back: The metanephric blastema (metanephric mesoderm).

subject: Anatomy
front: The collecting system, trigone and posterior wall of the urethra develop from what?
back: The mesonephric duct (via the ureteric bud).

subject: Anatomy
front: The urinary bladder, urethra and prostate develop from what?
back: The urogenital sinus (endoderm).

subject: Anatomy
front: From which mesoderm does the whole urogenital system develop?
back: Intermediate mesoderm.

subject: Anatomy
front: What germ layer forms each part of the pharyngeal apparatus — cleft, arch, pouch?
back: Cleft – ectoderm; arch – mesoderm + neural crest; pouch – endoderm.

subject: Anatomy
front: What does the 1st pharyngeal cleft form?
back: External auditory meatus and the (outer) tympanic membrane.

subject: Anatomy
front: What happens to the 2nd–4th pharyngeal clefts, and what can persist?
back: They form the cervical sinus, which normally obliterates; if it persists → a branchial cyst/sinus (at the anterior border of SCM, upper 2/3).

subject: Anatomy
front: What does the 1st pharyngeal pouch form?
back: Middle ear cavity, Eustachian (auditory) tube, mastoid antrum, and the (inner) tympanic membrane.

subject: Anatomy
front: What does the 2nd pharyngeal pouch form?
back: Epithelial lining of the crypts of the palatine tonsil (including the crypta magna).

subject: Anatomy
front: What does the 3rd pharyngeal pouch form (mnemonic 'THird')?
back: Thymus and the inferior parathyroid glands.

subject: Anatomy
front: What does the 4th pharyngeal pouch form?
back: Superior parathyroid glands and the ultimobranchial body (5th-arch remnant → parafollicular C cells).

subject: Anatomy
front: DiGeorge syndrome results from a defect of which pouches, and which chromosomal deletion?
back: The 3rd and 4th pharyngeal pouches (3rd > 4th); a 22q11 microdeletion.

subject: Anatomy
front: List the features of DiGeorge syndrome (CATCH-22).
back: Cardiac abnormalities; Abnormal facies; Thymic aplasia → T-cell defect with recurrent infections; Cleft palate/Choanal atresia; Hypocalcaemia (parathyroid/PTH hypoplasia); 22q deletion.

subject: Anatomy
front: 1st pharyngeal arch — cartilage, bones, muscles, nerve, artery?
back: Meckel's cartilage; Maxilla, Mandible, Malleus & incus, zygomatic bone, sphenomandibular ligament; muscles of mastication, mylohyoid, anterior belly of digastric, tensor veli palatini; nerve V3; maxillary artery.

subject: Anatomy
front: 2nd pharyngeal arch — cartilage, bones, muscles, nerve, artery?
back: Reichert's cartilage; Stapes, styloid process, lesser horn & upper body of hyoid, stylohyoid ligament; muscles of facial expression, stylohyoid, stapedius, buccinator, platysma, posterior belly of digastric; nerve VII; stapedial artery.

subject: Anatomy
front: 3rd pharyngeal arch — bone, muscle, nerve, artery?
back: Hyoid bone (body & greater horn); stylopharyngeus; nerve IX; common carotid + internal carotid artery.

subject: Anatomy
front: 4th pharyngeal arch — cartilage, muscles, nerve, artery?
back: Laryngeal cartilages; cricothyroid, levator veli palatini, pharyngeal constrictors; nerve = vagus (X) via the superior laryngeal nerve; artery = left → aortic arch, right → proximal right subclavian artery (+ 7th intersegmental artery + dorsal aorta).

subject: Anatomy
front: 6th pharyngeal arch — muscles, nerve, artery?
back: All intrinsic laryngeal muscles (except cricothyroid) + cricopharyngeus; nerve = vagus (X) via the recurrent laryngeal nerve; artery = pulmonary artery, and (on the left) the ductus arteriosus.

subject: Anatomy
front: Why does the left recurrent laryngeal nerve take a longer/different course?
back: It hooks around the ligamentum arteriosum (a left 6th-arch derivative).

subject: Anatomy
front: All palatal muscles are supplied by which nerve, and what is the exception (per the notes)?
back: SLN (X) — except tensor veli palatini, which is supplied by V3.

subject: Anatomy
front: All pharyngeal muscles are supplied by which nerve, and what is the exception (per the notes)?
back: SLN (X) — except cricopharyngeus. (Stylopharyngeus is supplied by IX.)

subject: Anatomy
front: All laryngeal muscles are supplied by which nerve, and what is the exception?
back: RLN (X) — except cricothyroid, supplied by the external branch of the SLN.

subject: Anatomy
front: Where is Killian's dehiscence, and what does it predispose to?
back: A posterolateral weak area between the thyropharyngeus and cricopharyngeus parts of the inferior pharyngeal constrictor; it is the site of Zenker's (pharyngeal pouch) diverticulum. Cricopharyngeus is supplied by the RLN.

subject: Anatomy
deck: Part-08: Embryology IV - Mesentery & Fetal Circulation
front: What are the derivatives of the ventral mesentery?
back: Lesser omentum (hepatogastric + hepatoduodenal ligaments), falciform ligament, triangular ligament of liver, and coronary ligament of liver.

subject: Anatomy
deck: Part-08: Embryology IV - Mesentery & Fetal Circulation
front: What does each part of the lesser omentum contain?
back: Hepatogastric ligament – right & left gastric arteries; hepatoduodenal ligament – the portal triad (portal vein, hepatic artery, bile duct).

subject: Anatomy
deck: Part-08: Embryology IV - Mesentery & Fetal Circulation
front: The falciform ligament contains which structure, and what is it a remnant of?
back: The ligamentum teres hepatis (round ligament) — the obliterated left umbilical vein.

subject: Anatomy
deck: Part-08: Embryology IV - Mesentery & Fetal Circulation
front: What are the derivatives of the dorsal mesentery?
back: Greater omentum, gastrosplenic ligament, lienorenal ligament, gastrophrenic ligament, mesentery of small intestine & mesoappendix, and transverse & sigmoid mesocolon.

subject: Anatomy
deck: Part-08: Embryology IV - Mesentery & Fetal Circulation
front: What does the gastrosplenic ligament contain?
back: Short gastric and left gastroepiploic vessels.

subject: Anatomy
deck: Part-08: Embryology IV - Mesentery & Fetal Circulation
front: What does the lienorenal (splenorenal) ligament contain?
back: The splenic vessels and the tail of the pancreas.

subject: Anatomy
deck: Part-08: Embryology IV - Mesentery & Fetal Circulation
front: What are the boundaries of the epiploic foramen (foramen of Winslow)?
back: Anterior – hepatoduodenal ligament (portal vein, hepatic artery, bile duct); posterior – IVC; superior – caudate lobe/process of liver; inferior – 1st part of duodenum.

subject: Anatomy
deck: Part-08: Embryology IV - Mesentery & Fetal Circulation
front: What is Pringle's manoeuvre, and what does continued bleeding indicate?
back: Compression of the hepatoduodenal ligament (portal triad) at the epiploic foramen to control liver bleeding; if bleeding continues, the source is the hepatic vein or IVC.

subject: Anatomy
deck: Part-08: Embryology IV - Mesentery & Fetal Circulation
front: What forms the anterior and posterior boundaries of the lesser sac (omental bursa)?
back: Anterior – lesser omentum, stomach, gastrocolic ligament; posterior – pancreas, upper part of left kidney, left adrenal gland and the diaphragm.

subject: Anatomy
deck: Part-08: Embryology IV - Mesentery & Fetal Circulation
front: Which ligament prevents descent of the spleen?
back: The phrenicocolic ligament.

subject: Anatomy
deck: Part-08: Embryology IV - Mesentery & Fetal Circulation
front: What does the MEDIAN umbilical ligament represent?
back: The remnant of the urachus (itself a remnant of the allantois).

subject: Anatomy
deck: Part-08: Embryology IV - Mesentery & Fetal Circulation
front: What does the MEDIAL umbilical ligament represent, and what does the proximal part remain as?
back: The obliterated distal umbilical artery; the proximal part persists as the superior vesical artery.

subject: Anatomy
deck: Part-08: Embryology IV - Mesentery & Fetal Circulation
front: What does the LATERAL umbilical fold contain?
back: The inferior epigastric artery and vein.

subject: Anatomy
deck: Part-08: Embryology IV - Mesentery & Fetal Circulation
front: The left umbilical vein becomes which adult structure?
back: The ligamentum teres hepatis (round ligament of the liver).

subject: Anatomy
deck: Part-08: Embryology IV - Mesentery & Fetal Circulation
front: A patent urachus causes what, and predisposes to which malignancy?
back: Urine discharge from the umbilicus; predisposes to adenocarcinoma of the bladder.

subject: Anatomy
deck: Part-08: Embryology IV - Mesentery & Fetal Circulation
front: What does the vitelline duct form as a remnant, and what does a patent duct cause?
back: Remnant → Meckel's diverticulum; a patent duct → faecal discharge from the umbilicus.

subject: Anatomy
deck: Part-08: Embryology IV - Mesentery & Fetal Circulation
front: Name the three fetal shunts.
back: Ductus venosus, foramen ovale, and ductus arteriosus.

subject: Anatomy
deck: Part-08: Embryology IV - Mesentery & Fetal Circulation
front: Ductus arteriosus — functional and anatomical closure times?
back: Functional 2–3 days (10–15 hours); anatomical 2–3 weeks.

subject: Anatomy
deck: Part-08: Embryology IV - Mesentery & Fetal Circulation
front: Foramen ovale — functional and anatomical closure times?
back: Functional within minutes; anatomical closure by one year.

subject: Anatomy
deck: Part-08: Embryology IV - Mesentery & Fetal Circulation
front: Ductus venosus — functional and anatomical closure times?
back: Functional within minutes; anatomical 3–7 days.

subject: Anatomy
deck: Part-08: Embryology IV - Mesentery & Fetal Circulation
front: Adult remnants of the three fetal shunts?
back: Ductus arteriosus → ligamentum arteriosum; foramen ovale → fossa ovalis; ductus venosus → ligamentum venosum.

subject: Anatomy
deck: Part-08: Embryology IV - Mesentery & Fetal Circulation
front: Which fetal vessel carries the most oxygenated (high O2) blood?
back: The umbilical vein (from the placenta); the umbilical arteries carry deoxygenated blood back to the placenta.

subject: Anatomy
deck: Part-08: Embryology IV - Mesentery & Fetal Circulation
front: Which embryonic veins form each segment of the IVC?
back: Hepatic segment – vitelline veins; suprarenal segment – subcardinal vein; renal segment – sub-supracardinal anastomosis; infrarenal segment – supracardinal vein.

subject: Anatomy
deck: Part-08: Embryology IV - Mesentery & Fetal Circulation
front: The supracardinal veins form which vessels (besides part of the IVC)?
back: The azygos and hemiazygos veins.

subject: Anatomy
deck: Part-08: Embryology IV - Mesentery & Fetal Circulation
front: What do the anterior and posterior cardinal veins form?
back: Anterior cardinal veins → brachiocephalic veins (and, with the right common cardinal vein, the SVC); posterior cardinal veins → common iliac veins.

subject: Anatomy
deck: Part-08: Embryology IV - Mesentery & Fetal Circulation
front: The vitelline veins give rise to which structures?
back: Portal vein, hepatic sinusoids, hepatic veins, and the hepatic segment of the IVC.

subject: Anatomy
deck: Part-08: Embryology IV - Mesentery & Fetal Circulation
front: What is an aberrant right subclavian artery (ARSA / arteria lusoria), and what does it cause?
back: An anomalous right subclavian artery that passes behind the oesophagus, causing a posterior oesophageal indentation → dysphagia lusoria.

subject: Anatomy
front: The truncus arteriosus gives rise to what, and what divides it?
back: The aorta and pulmonary artery/trunk — divided by the spiral (aorticopulmonary) septum, a neural-crest derivative.

subject: Anatomy
front: What does the conus cordis form?
back: The smooth outflow tracts (RVOT and LVOT).

subject: Anatomy
front: What does the bulbus cordis (proximal part) form?
back: The trabeculated (rough) part of the right ventricle.

subject: Anatomy
front: What does the primitive ventricle form?
back: The trabeculated part of the left ventricle.

subject: Anatomy
front: What does the primitive atrium form?
back: The trabeculated (pectinate) parts of the right and left atria.

subject: Anatomy
front: Sinus venosus – body & right horn (septum spurium) form what?
back: The smooth part of the right atrium (sinus venarum).

subject: Anatomy
front: Sinus venosus – left horn forms what?
back: The coronary sinus.

subject: Anatomy
front: The common cardinal vein forms what?
back: The SVC (partly from the anterior cardinal vein).

subject: Anatomy
front: The primitive pulmonary vein forms what?
back: The smooth part of the left atrium.

subject: Anatomy
front: Spiral (aorticopulmonary) septum defects: anterior deviation, absence, and failure to spiral?
back: Anterior deviation → Tetralogy of Fallot (overriding aorta); absence → persistent truncus arteriosus (PTA); failure to spiral → transposition of great arteries (TGA; RV→aorta, LV→PA).

subject: Anatomy
front: What does the crista terminalis separate in the right atrium?
back: The smooth part (sinus venarum) from the rough/pectinate part.

subject: Anatomy
front: What is the valve of the IVC opening called?
back: The Eustachian valve (the IVC opening is valved).

subject: Anatomy
front: What is the valve of the coronary sinus called?
back: The Thebesian valve.

subject: Anatomy
front: Is the SVC opening valved or valveless?
back: Valveless.

subject: Anatomy
front: What does the Triangle of Koch contain?
back: The AV node (the bundle of His runs from its apex).

subject: Anatomy
front: What are the boundaries of the Triangle of Koch?
back: (1) Septal leaflet of the tricuspid valve, (2) tendon of Todaro, (3) ostium of the coronary sinus.

subject: Anatomy
front: What does the moderator band carry, and where is it?
back: The right bundle branch — in the right ventricle, connecting to the anterior papillary muscle.

subject: Anatomy
front: Name the papillary muscles of the right ventricle.
back: Anterior (largest), posterior, and septal.

subject: Anatomy
front: What bounds the foramen ovale (atrial septum)?
back: Septum secundum (upper) and septum primum, whose lower flap acts as the valve; the ostium secundum forms within the septum primum.

subject: Anatomy
front: What passes through the diaphragm at T8?
back: The IVC and the right phrenic nerve. (Mnemonic 'I ate/8'.)

subject: Anatomy
front: What passes through the diaphragm at T10?
back: The oesophagus, oesophageal branches of the left gastric artery, and the vagus nerves (vagal trunks). (Mnemonic '10 eggs'.)

subject: Anatomy
front: What passes through the diaphragm at T12?
back: The aorta, thoracic duct, and azygos vein. (Mnemonic 'Aorta, Azygos, Thoracic duct — AAT 12'.)

subject: Anatomy
front: What is the nerve supply of the diaphragm?
back: The phrenic nerve (C3, C4, C5).

subject: Anatomy
front: What forms the central tendon of the diaphragm?
back: The septum transversum.

subject: Anatomy
front: What forms the crura / dorsal part of the diaphragm?
back: The dorsal mesentery of the oesophagus.

subject: Anatomy
front: Failure of the pleuroperitoneal membranes causes what?
back: Congenital diaphragmatic hernia (CDH).

subject: Anatomy
front: What forms the peripheral muscular part of the diaphragm?
back: The lateral (body-wall) chest-wall somites.

subject: Anatomy
front: Normal gut rotation — degree, direction, and axis?
back: 270° anticlockwise around the axis of the superior mesenteric artery (SMA).

subject: Anatomy
front: When does physiological (umbilical) herniation of the gut occur?
back: At 9–11 weeks.

subject: Anatomy
front: Non-rotation (90° only) — result?
back: Small bowel on the right, large bowel on the left.

subject: Anatomy
front: Reverse rotation — result?
back: Duodenum passes anterior to the transverse colon → large bowel obstruction.

subject: Anatomy
front: Mixed rotation — result?
back: Subpyloric caecum with Ladd bands → duodenal obstruction ('double bubble'); risk of midgut volvulus.

subject: Anatomy
front: Incomplete rotation — result?
back: Subhepatic caecum.

subject: Anatomy
front: Embryological cause of an aberrant right subclavian artery (ARSA / dysphagia lusoria)?
back: Regression of the right 4th aortic arch with persistence of the caudal part of the right dorsal aorta → retro-oesophageal course → posterior oesophageal indentation.

subject: Anatomy
front: Embryological cause of a double aortic arch?
back: Persistence of the right 4th arch + caudal part of the right dorsal aorta (failure to regress) → vascular ring.

subject: Anatomy
front: How is the azygos vein formed and where does it drain?
back: Formed by the right ascending lumbar + right subcostal veins; drains into the SVC by arching over the right main bronchus at ~T4.

subject: Anatomy
front: Tributaries of the hemiazygos and accessory hemiazygos veins?
back: Hemiazygos (left ascending lumbar + left subcostal) receives the left 9th–11th posterior intercostal veins; accessory hemiazygos receives the left 5th–8th posterior intercostal veins; both cross to the azygos.

subject: Anatomy
front: Where does the thoracic duct begin and end, and what levels does it cross?
back: Begins at the cisterna chyli (L2); crosses to the left at ~T5; drains at the junction of the left internal jugular & left subclavian veins (root of neck, C7).

subject: Anatomy
front: What does the right lymphatic duct drain?
back: The right side of the head & neck, the right thorax, and the right upper limb (right jugular, subclavian & bronchomediastinal trunks).

subject: Anatomy
front: Coracoid process — what type of epiphysis is it?
back: Atavistic.

subject: Anatomy
front: Coracoid process — muscle origins and insertion?
back: Origin of the short head of biceps and coracobrachialis; insertion of pectoralis minor.

subject: Anatomy
front: Pressure epiphyses — examples?
back: Head of femur/humerus, lower end of radius, tibial condyles.

subject: Anatomy
front: Traction epiphyses — examples?
back: Greater & lesser trochanters of femur, mastoid process, greater & lesser tubercles of humerus.

subject: Anatomy
front: Atavistic epiphyses — examples?
back: Coracoid process and os trigonum.

subject: Anatomy
front: Aberrant epiphyses — examples?
back: Head of the 1st metacarpal and bases of the other metacarpals.

subject: Anatomy
front: Name the rotator cuff muscles and their attachments (SITS).
back: Greater tubercle – Supraspinatus, Infraspinatus, Teres minor; Lesser tubercle – Subscapularis ('forgotten tendon').

subject: Anatomy
front: Actions of the rotator cuff muscles?
back: Supraspinatus – initiates abduction (0–15°); infraspinatus & teres minor – external rotation; subscapularis – internal rotation + adduction.

subject: Anatomy
front: Nerve supply of the rotator cuff muscles?
back: Supraspinatus & infraspinatus – suprascapular nerve; teres minor – axillary nerve; subscapularis – upper & lower subscapular nerves.

subject: Anatomy
front: Which muscles attach at the intertubercular (bicipital) groove?
back: Lateral lip – pectoralis major; floor – latissimus dorsi; medial lip – teres major ('a lady between two majors').

subject: Anatomy
front: Bicipital-groove muscles — actions and nerves?
back: Pectoralis major – flexion (lateral & medial pectoral nerves); latissimus dorsi – extension (thoracodorsal nerve); teres major – extension (lower subscapular nerve).

subject: Anatomy
front: What does the clavipectoral fascia enclose, and what pierces it (TLC)?
back: Encloses subclavius and pectoralis minor; pierced by the Thoracoacromial artery, Lateral pectoral nerve and Cephalic vein.

subject: Anatomy
front: Which nerve pierces pectoralis minor itself?
back: The medial pectoral nerve.

subject: Anatomy
front: Roots of the brachial plexus and its five components in order?
back: C5–T1; roots → trunks → divisions → cords → branches.

subject: Anatomy
front: The trunks of the brachial plexus and their roots?
back: Upper (C5–C6), middle (C7), lower (C8–T1).

subject: Anatomy
front: Branches from the roots of the brachial plexus?
back: Dorsal scapular nerve (C5) and long thoracic nerve (C5,6,7).

subject: Anatomy
front: Branches from the upper trunk?
back: Suprascapular nerve and the nerve to subclavius.

subject: Anatomy
front: Lateral cord branches (mnemonic LML)?
back: Lateral pectoral nerve, Musculocutaneous nerve, Lateral root of the median nerve.

subject: Anatomy
front: Posterior cord branches (mnemonic ULTRA)?
back: Upper subscapular, Lower subscapular, Thoracodorsal, Radial and Axillary nerves.

subject: Anatomy
front: Medial cord branches (mnemonic M4U)?
back: Medial pectoral nerve, Medial brachial cutaneous nerve, Medial antebrachial cutaneous nerve, medial root of the Median, and Ulnar nerve.

subject: Anatomy
front: Where do the divisions of the brachial plexus lie, and do they give branches?
back: Retroclavicular (behind the clavicle); they give no branches.

subject: Anatomy
front: Musculocutaneous nerve — root value and what it supplies?
back: C5,6,7; supplies biceps, brachialis and coracobrachialis; continues as the lateral cutaneous nerve of the forearm.

subject: Anatomy
front: Axillary nerve — root value and what it supplies?
back: C5,6; supplies deltoid & teres minor; gives the upper lateral cutaneous nerve of the arm.

subject: Anatomy
front: Dorsal scapular nerve supplies which muscles?
back: Levator scapulae, rhomboid minor and rhomboid major.

subject: Anatomy
front: Thoracodorsal nerve (LAD) — root value, muscle and actions?
back: C6,7,8; latissimus dorsi; climbing, adduction, internal rotation and extension.

subject: Anatomy
front: Erb's palsy — roots and deformity?
back: Upper trunk (C5–C6); arm adducted & internally rotated, forearm extended & pronated ('waiter's tip').

subject: Anatomy
front: Klumpke's palsy — roots and deformity?
back: Lower trunk (C8–T1); claw hand.

subject: Anatomy
front: Medial winging of the scapula — nerve and muscle?
back: Long thoracic nerve → serratus anterior.

subject: Anatomy
front: Lateral winging of the scapula — nerve(s)/muscle(s)?
back: Spinal accessory nerve (trapezius) or dorsal scapular nerve (rhomboids).

subject: Anatomy
front: Subclavian artery branches (mnemonic VIT C&D)?
back: Vertebral, Internal thoracic, Thyrocervical trunk, Costocervical trunk, Dorsal scapular.

subject: Anatomy
front: Branches of the thyrocervical trunk?
back: Inferior thyroid, transverse cervical (→ trapezius, SCM) and suprascapular arteries.

subject: Anatomy
front: Branches of the costocervical trunk?
back: Deep cervical and highest (supreme) intercostal arteries.

subject: Anatomy
front: Parts of the subclavian artery relative to scalenus anterior?
back: 1st part medial (vertebral, internal thoracic, thyrocervical); 2nd part posterior (costocervical); 3rd part lateral (dorsal scapular).

subject: Anatomy
front: What lies anterior to scalenus anterior?
back: The phrenic nerve and the subclavian vein.

subject: Anatomy
front: What passes through the interscalene triangle (between scalenus anterior & medius)?
back: The subclavian artery and the roots of the brachial plexus.

subject: Anatomy
front: How is the axillary artery divided, and how many branches per part?
back: Divided by pectoralis minor: 1st part (1 branch), 2nd part (2 branches), 3rd part (3 branches).

subject: Anatomy
front: Branch of the 1st part of the axillary artery?
back: Superior thoracic artery.

subject: Anatomy
front: Branches of the 2nd part of the axillary artery?
back: Thoracoacromial and lateral thoracic arteries.

subject: Anatomy
front: Branches of the 3rd part of the axillary artery?
back: Subscapular, anterior circumflex humeral and posterior circumflex humeral arteries.

subject: Anatomy
front: What passes through the quadrangular space?
back: The axillary nerve and the posterior circumflex humeral artery.

subject: Anatomy
front: Walls of the axilla?
back: Anterior – pectoralis major & minor; posterior – subscapularis, teres major, latissimus dorsi; medial – serratus anterior/chest wall; lateral – bicipital groove of the humerus.

subject: Anatomy
front: Contents of the axilla?
back: Axillary artery, axillary vein, cords of the brachial plexus (and lymph nodes).

subject: Anatomy
front: The axillary sheath is a continuation of which fascia?
back: The prevertebral fascia.

subject: Anatomy
front: Lower borders of lung vs pleura at the midclavicular, midaxillary and paravertebral lines?
back: Midclavicular – lung 6th / pleura 8th rib; midaxillary – lung 8th / pleura 10th; paravertebral – lung 10th / pleura 12th.

subject: Anatomy
front: Order of the intercostal neurovascular bundle in the costal groove?
back: Vein, Artery, Nerve (VAN) from above downward.

subject: Anatomy
front: Number of bronchopulmonary segments in each lung?
back: Right lung – 10; left lung – 8/9/10.

subject: Anatomy
front: The two pericardial sinuses?
back: Transverse sinus – between the arterial (aorta/PA) and venous (SVC) ends; oblique sinus – a cul-de-sac behind the left atrium.

subject: Anatomy
front: Arrangement of structures at the lung hilum (anterior to posterior)?
back: Pulmonary vein (anterior), pulmonary artery, bronchus (posterior) – 'VAB'; on the right the eparterial bronchus lies above the artery, on the left the bronchus is hyparterial.

subject: Anatomy
front: Relation of the phrenic and vagus nerves to the root of the lung?
back: Phrenic nerve passes anterior; vagus nerve passes posterior to the hilum.

subject: Anatomy
front: Why is the right dome of the diaphragm higher, and which hilum/kidney sits higher?
back: Right diaphragm higher (liver); left hilum higher; left kidney higher (right kidney pushed down by the liver).

subject: Anatomy
front: Greater sciatic foramen — structure ABOVE piriformis?
back: Superior gluteal nerve and artery.

subject: Anatomy
front: Greater sciatic foramen — structures BELOW piriformis?
back: Inferior gluteal nerve & artery, sciatic nerve, posterior femoral cutaneous nerve, and the pudendal neurovascular bundle.

subject: Anatomy
front: Which structures wrap around the ischial spine (mnemonic PIN, medial→lateral)?
back: Pudendal nerve, Internal pudendal artery, Nerve to obturator internus.

subject: Anatomy
front: What muscles make up the pelvic diaphragm?
back: Levator ani (pubococcygeus, puborectalis, iliococcygeus) + coccygeus.

subject: Anatomy
front: Pubococcygeus slings around which structures, and what results from weakness?
back: Urethra (puborethralis), vagina (pubovaginalis), rectum (puborectalis); weakness of all three → prolapse.

subject: Anatomy
front: Contents of the ischiorectal fossa?
back: (1) Pudendal nerve, (2) internal pudendal vessels, (3) inferior rectal nerve & vessels, (4) posterior scrotal nerves & vessels, (5) perforating cutaneous nerve (S2, S3).

subject: Anatomy
front: Why can an ischiorectal abscess be 'horseshoe'?
back: The two ischiorectal fossae communicate posteriorly, allowing a horseshoe abscess.

subject: Anatomy
front: Contents of the deep perineal pouch?
back: Sphincter urethrae (external urethral sphincter), deep transverse perinei, membranous urethra, and bulbourethral (Cowper's) glands.

subject: Anatomy
front: Which muscles attach to the perineal body?
back: Paired: bulbospongiosus, superficial & deep transverse perinei, levator ani; unpaired: external anal sphincter and longitudinal muscle of the anal canal.

subject: Anatomy
front: Contents of the spermatic cord (rule of 3)?
back: 3 arteries (testicular, cremasteric, artery to vas); 3 nerves (genital branch of genitofemoral, autonomic/sympathetic, + ilioinguinal running alongside); 3 fascia (external spermatic, cremasteric, internal spermatic); 3 others (vas deferens, pampiniform plexus, lymphatics + processus vaginalis remnant).

subject: Anatomy
front: Where does the ilioinguinal nerve run relative to the spermatic cord?
back: Within the inguinal canal but OUTSIDE (not within) the spermatic cord.

subject: Anatomy
front: Muscle/structure attaching to each pelvic landmark (iliac crest, ASIS, AIIS, greater & lesser trochanter, ischial tuberosity, pubic symphysis)?
back: Iliac crest – abdominal muscles; ASIS – sartorius; AIIS – rectus femoris; greater trochanter – gluteus medius & minimus; lesser trochanter – iliopsoas; ischial tuberosity – hamstrings; pubic symphysis – adductors.

subject: Anatomy
front: Nerve supply of the thigh compartments?
back: Anterior – femoral nerve; medial – obturator nerve; posterior – sciatic nerve.

subject: Anatomy
front: Gluteus maximus — action and nerve supply?
back: Main extensor + abduction/external rotation (ABER) of the hip; inferior gluteal nerve.

subject: Anatomy
front: Gluteus medius & minimus — action and nerve?
back: Abduction + internal rotation of the hip; superior gluteal nerve.

subject: Anatomy
front: Trendelenburg sign — which nerve, and what happens?
back: Superior gluteal nerve palsy weakens the abductors → the pelvis droops on the opposite (contralateral) side; the patient lurches to the affected side.

subject: Anatomy
front: Gower's sign is associated with which condition?
back: Duchenne muscular dystrophy (weak gluteus maximus).

subject: Anatomy
front: Nerve to obturator internus and nerve to quadratus femoris each also supply which muscle?
back: Nerve to obturator internus → superior gemellus; nerve to quadratus femoris → inferior gemellus.

subject: Anatomy
front: The iliotibial tract receives which muscles and inserts where?
back: Gluteus maximus + tensor fasciae latae; inserts at Gerdy's tubercle on the lateral tibial condyle.

subject: Anatomy
front: Which muscle is the 'tailor's muscle' and which is the 'footballer's muscle'?
back: Sartorius = tailor's muscle; rectus femoris = footballer's muscle (hip flexion + knee extension).

subject: Anatomy
front: What is piriformis syndrome?
back: Compression of the sciatic nerve by the piriformis muscle.

subject: Anatomy
front: Boundaries of the femoral triangle?
back: Superior – inguinal ligament; lateral – sartorius; medial – adductor longus; floor – iliopsoas & pectineus.

subject: Anatomy
front: Contents of the femoral triangle, lateral to medial (NAVEL)?
back: Femoral Nerve, femoral Artery, femoral Vein, Empty space (femoral canal), Lymphatics (Cloquet's node).

subject: Anatomy
front: What does the femoral sheath enclose, and what is in the femoral canal?
back: Encloses the femoral artery, vein and canal (NOT the femoral nerve, which lies outside); the femoral canal contains Cloquet's lymph node.

subject: Anatomy
front: Boundaries of the popliteal fossa?
back: Superolateral – biceps femoris; superomedial – semitendinosus & semimembranosus; inferior – the two heads of gastrocnemius.

subject: Anatomy
front: Contents of the popliteal fossa (superficial to deep) and the deepest structure?
back: Tibial nerve & common peroneal nerve (superficial) → popliteal vein → popliteal artery (deepest).

subject: Anatomy
front: Contents of the cubital fossa (mnemonic MBTR, medial to lateral)?
back: Median nerve, Brachial artery, biceps Tendon, Radial nerve; boundaries – pronator teres (medial), brachioradialis (lateral).

subject: Anatomy
front: Upper limb dermatomes C5–T1?
back: C5 – lateral antecubital fossa; C6 – thumb; C7 – middle finger; C8 – little finger; T1 – medial antecubital fossa.

subject: Anatomy
front: Lower limb dermatomes L2–S1?
back: L2 – mid anterior thigh; L3 – knee; L4 – medial aspect of calf; L5 – dorsum of foot & lateral calf; S1 – sole & lateral foot.

subject: Anatomy
front: Myotomes (motor) for L2, L3, L4, L5, S1?
back: L2 – hip flexion; L3 – knee extension; L4 – ankle dorsiflexion; L5 – great-toe extension (EHL); S1 – plantarflexion (FHL).

subject: Anatomy
front: Root values of the reflexes: knee jerk, ankle jerk, cremasteric, bulbocavernosus?
back: Knee jerk L3–L4; ankle jerk S1–S2; cremasteric L1–L2; bulbocavernosus S3–S4–S5 (first reflex to recover after spinal shock).

subject: Anatomy
front: Where are the dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses felt?
back: Dorsalis pedis – dorsum of foot (continuation of the anterior tibial artery); posterior tibial – behind the medial malleolus.

subject: Anatomy
front: Conus vs cauda — vertebral level?
back: Conus L1–L2; cauda L2–sacrum.

subject: Anatomy
front: Conus vs cauda — UMN/LMN pattern?
back: Conus: UMN + LMN (sacral cord segment & roots); cauda: LMN only (lumbosacral roots).

subject: Anatomy
front: Conus vs cauda — onset/presentation?
back: Conus: sudden and bilateral; cauda: gradual and unilateral.

subject: Anatomy
front: Conus vs cauda — radicular pain?
back: Conus: less severe; cauda: more severe.

subject: Anatomy
front: Conus vs cauda — motor strength?
back: Conus: symmetrical; cauda: asymmetric.

subject: Anatomy
front: Conus vs cauda — sensory loss?
back: Conus: symmetrical bilateral perianal loss; cauda: localized saddle numbness, asymmetrical/unilateral.

subject: Anatomy
front: Conus vs cauda — reflexes affected?
back: Conus: ankle jerks; cauda: both knee and ankle jerks.

subject: Anatomy
front: Conus vs cauda — sphincter dysfunction?
back: Conus: early urinary & faecal incontinence (Onuf's nucleus); cauda: tends to present late.

subject: Anatomy
front: Conus vs cauda — impotence?
back: Conus: frequent; cauda: less frequent.

subject: Anatomy
front: Structures behind the medial malleolus under the flexor retinaculum (Tom, Dick, And Very Nervous Harry)?
back: Tibialis posterior, Flexor Digitorum Longus, posterior tibial Artery, posterior tibial Vein, posterior tibial Nerve, Flexor Hallucis Longus.

subject: Anatomy
front: Tarsal tunnel syndrome compresses which nerve?
back: The posterior tibial nerve (under the flexor retinaculum).

subject: Anatomy
front: Order of structures under the extensor retinaculum (medial to lateral)?
back: Tibialis anterior, EHL, dorsalis pedis/anterior tibial artery, deep peroneal nerve, EDL.

subject: Anatomy
front: Nerve of each leg compartment?
back: Anterior – deep peroneal nerve; lateral – superficial peroneal nerve; posterior – posterior tibial nerve.

subject: Anatomy
front: Insertions of peroneus longus, brevis and tertius?
back: Peroneus longus – base of 1st metatarsal & medial cuneiform; peroneus brevis – base of 5th metatarsal; peroneus tertius – base (dorsum) of 5th metatarsal.

subject: Anatomy
front: Jones vs pseudo-Jones fracture of the 5th metatarsal?
back: Jones fracture – shaft/base of the 5th metatarsal; pseudo-Jones (avulsion) fracture – tuberosity of the 5th metatarsal (peroneus brevis pull).

subject: Anatomy
front: Lateral collateral ligament of the ankle — parts and the one most commonly injured?
back: Anterior talofibular, calcaneofibular and posterior talofibular ligaments; the anterior talofibular ligament is most commonly injured (inversion sprain).

subject: Anatomy
front: What does the spring (plantar calcaneonavicular) ligament support?
back: The head of the talus and the medial longitudinal arch.

subject: Anatomy
front: Muscles of the 1st layer of the sole?
back: Abductor hallucis, flexor digitorum brevis, abductor digiti minimi.

subject: Anatomy
front: Contents of the 2nd layer of the sole?
back: Quadratus plantae and the lumbricals (plus the tendons of FHL and FDL).

subject: Anatomy
front: Muscles of the 3rd layer of the sole?
back: Flexor hallucis brevis, adductor hallucis (oblique & transverse heads), flexor digiti minimi brevis.

subject: Anatomy
front: Contents of the 4th layer of the sole?
back: The interossei (plantar & dorsal) plus the tendons of peroneus longus and tibialis posterior.

subject: Anatomy
front: Where does the plantar arterial arch lie?
back: Between the 3rd and 4th layers of the sole.

subject: Anatomy
front: What are the three branches of the coeliac trunk?
back: Left gastric, splenic, and common hepatic arteries.

subject: Anatomy
front: Branches of the splenic artery?
back: Pancreatica magna, short gastric arteries (to the fundus), and the left gastroepiploic artery.

subject: Anatomy
front: The left gastric artery gives which additional branch?
back: Oesophageal branches.

subject: Anatomy
front: Branches of the common hepatic artery?
back: Gastroduodenal artery, right gastric artery, and the proper hepatic artery (→ right & left hepatic arteries).

subject: Anatomy
front: The gastroduodenal artery gives which branches?
back: Right gastroepiploic and superior pancreaticoduodenal arteries.

subject: Anatomy
front: The cystic artery is a branch of which artery?
back: The right hepatic artery.

subject: Anatomy
front: The fundus of the stomach is supplied by which arteries?
back: The short gastric arteries (from the splenic artery).

subject: Anatomy
front: Where does the gastroduodenal artery lie, and what is its clinical significance?
back: Posterior to the 1st part of the duodenum; a posterior duodenal ulcer can erode it → GI bleeding.

subject: Anatomy
front: The superior pancreaticoduodenal artery anastomoses with which artery, forming what?
back: The inferior pancreaticoduodenal artery (from the SMA), forming the pancreaticoduodenal arcade (a foregut–midgut anastomosis).

subject: Anatomy
front: Branches of the POSTERIOR division of the internal iliac artery (mnemonic SIL)?
back: Superior gluteal, Iliolumbar, and Lateral sacral arteries.

subject: Anatomy
front: Branches of the ANTERIOR division of the internal iliac artery?
back: Superior & inferior vesical, obturator, uterine, vaginal, middle rectal, inferior gluteal, and internal pudendal arteries (plus the umbilical artery).

subject: Anatomy
front: What are the anterior and posterior perirenal fascia called?
back: Anterior – Gerota's fascia; posterior – Zuckerkandl's fascia.

subject: Anatomy
front: What structures are pierced to reach the kidney from behind?
back: Quadratus lumborum/erector spinae, the three layers of the thoracolumbar fascia, and the posterior renal fascia.

subject: Anatomy
front: Which Couinaud segment is the caudate lobe, and why does it hypertrophy in Budd-Chiari syndrome?
back: Segment I; it drains directly into the IVC (independent of the hepatic veins), so it is spared and hypertrophies in Budd-Chiari (hepatic vein obstruction).

subject: Anatomy
front: Which Couinaud segments form the right and left functional lobes of the liver?
back: Right lobe – V, VI, VII, VIII; left lobe – II, III, IV; caudate = I.

subject: Anatomy
front: The falciform ligament separates which liver segments?
back: Segment IV (medial) from segments II/III (lateral).

subject: Anatomy
front: What is Cantlie's line?
back: The principal plane from the IVC to the gallbladder fossa (along the middle hepatic vein) that separates the functional right and left lobes of the liver.

subject: Anatomy
front: Fibrocartilage — collagen type, perichondrium, and locations?
back: Collagen type I; NO perichondrium; found in IVD, symphyses, labrum, menisci and articular discs.

subject: Anatomy
front: Hyaline cartilage — collagen type, perichondrium, and locations?
back: Collagen type II; has a perichondrium (except articular cartilage); found in cricoid, thyroid, arytenoid, trachea and articular surfaces.

subject: Anatomy
front: Elastic cartilage — key feature, collagen type, and locations?
back: Elastin-rich, collagen type II, does NOT calcify; found in epiglottis, cuneiform, corniculate, eustachian tube, pinna and EAC (3 in the ear + 3 in the larynx).

subject: Anatomy
front: Which cartilages lack a perichondrium?
back: Fibrocartilage and articular (hyaline) cartilage.

subject: Anatomy
front: Merocrine, apocrine and holocrine secretion — how does each release product?
back: Merocrine – exocytosis (intact cell); apocrine – apical portion pinched off; holocrine – the whole cell disintegrates.

subject: Anatomy
front: Examples of apocrine glands?
back: Mammary gland, Moll's glands (eyelids), and ceruminous glands (EAC).

subject: Anatomy
front: Example of a holocrine gland?
back: The sebaceous gland.

subject: Anatomy
front: Which glands are purely serous, and which are mixed?
back: Serous – parotid and pancreas; mixed (serous + mucous) – submandibular and sublingual.

subject: Anatomy
front: How is the parotid distinguished from the pancreas histologically?
back: The parotid has only serous acini and NO islets; the pancreas has serous acini + islets.

subject: Anatomy
front: Which muscle has intercalated discs (gap junctions), and its other features?
back: Cardiac muscle – intercalated discs, central nuclei and branching striated fibres.

subject: Anatomy
front: Epithelium of the mouth and oesophagus?
back: Non-keratinized stratified squamous.

subject: Anatomy
front: Epithelium of the stomach and small intestine?
back: Simple columnar.

subject: Anatomy
front: Distinguishing histology of the duodenum, jejunum and ileum?
back: Duodenum – broad villi + Brunner's glands (submucosal); jejunum – tongue-shaped villi; ileum – flat villi + Peyer's patches.

subject: Anatomy
front: Histology of the large intestine?
back: Simple columnar with abundant goblet cells and no villi.

subject: Anatomy
front: Anal canal epithelium above vs below the pectinate line?
back: Above – simple columnar; below – stratified squamous.

subject: Anatomy
front: What are Paneth cells — location and function?
back: At the base of the intestinal crypts; they are zinc-rich with an immunological (antimicrobial) function.

subject: Anatomy
front: Epithelium of the trachea, bronchi and proximal bronchioles?
back: Pseudostratified ciliated columnar (respiratory epithelium).

subject: Anatomy
front: Epithelium of the terminal bronchiole?
back: Ciliated simple columnar/cuboidal.

subject: Anatomy
front: Epithelium of the respiratory bronchiole?
back: Simple columnar/cuboidal.

subject: Anatomy
front: Epithelium of the alveolus?
back: Simple squamous.

subject: Anatomy
front: Epithelium of the PCT and DCT?
back: Simple cuboidal (the PCT has a brush border).

subject: Anatomy
front: Epithelium of the calyces, ureter and bladder?
back: Transitional epithelium (urothelium) with umbrella cells.

subject: Anatomy
front: Epithelium of the epididymis and vas deferens?
back: Pseudostratified columnar with stereocilia.

subject: Anatomy
front: Epithelium of the ovary surface, fallopian tube, uterus/endocervix, and ectocervix/vagina?
back: Ovary – simple cuboidal (germinal epithelium); fallopian tube – ciliated simple columnar; uterus & endocervix – simple columnar; ectocervix & vagina – stratified squamous.

subject: Anatomy
front: The transformation zone (endocervix–ectocervix junction) is the site of what?
back: Cervical carcinoma.

subject: Anatomy
front: Corpora amylacea are found in which organ?
back: The prostate gland.

subject: Anatomy
front: Lymph node — key histological features?
back: Cortex, medulla, lymphoid follicles, subcapsular sinus and paracortex.

subject: Anatomy
front: Thymus — key histological features?
back: Cortex + medulla, NO follicles, prominent trabeculae/septa (Hassall's corpuscles in the medulla).

subject: Anatomy
front: Tonsil — key histological features?
back: No cortex/medulla distinction; has follicles and prominent epithelium-lined crypts.

subject: Anatomy
front: Spleen — key histological features?
back: White pulp and red pulp (no cortex/medulla), with connective-tissue trabeculae.

subject: Anatomy
front: Types of fibrous joints (synarthrosis), mnemonic SSG?
back: Sutures, Syndesmosis, Gomphosis.

subject: Anatomy
front: Types of sutures with examples?
back: Plain (frontonasal), serrate (interparietal), squamous (temporoparietal), denticulate (lambdoid), and schindylesis (sphenoid–vomer, wedge-and-groove).

subject: Anatomy
front: What is a syndesmosis, and what ossifies it?
back: A fibrous joint = interosseous membrane; it ossifies in fluorosis.

subject: Anatomy
front: What is a gomphosis?
back: A peg-in-socket fibrous joint (tooth in its socket, held by the periodontal ligament).

subject: Anatomy
front: Cartilaginous joints — primary vs secondary?
back: Synchondrosis (primary) = hyaline cartilage; symphysis (secondary) = fibrocartilage.

subject: Anatomy
front: Examples of synchondrosis (primary cartilaginous joints)?
back: Epiphyseal growth plate, 1st sternocostal joint, costochondral junction, spheno-occipital synchondrosis.

subject: Anatomy
front: Examples of symphysis (secondary cartilaginous joints)?
back: IVD, pubic symphysis, manubriosternal joint, xiphisternal joint.

subject: Anatomy
front: Ball-and-socket joints — examples?
back: Incus-stapes, hip, shoulder, talocalcaneonavicular.

subject: Anatomy
front: Saddle joints — examples?
back: Malleus-incus, 1st carpometacarpal, calcaneocuboid, sternoclavicular.

subject: Anatomy
front: Ellipsoid (condyloid) joint — example and movement?
back: Atlanto-occipital joint ('yes' nodding).

subject: Anatomy
front: Pivot joint — example and movement?
back: Atlanto-axial joint ('no' head-shaking).

subject: Anatomy
front: Condylar joints — examples?
back: TMJ and knee.

subject: Anatomy
front: Hinge joints — examples?
back: Ankle, elbow, interphalangeal joints.

subject: Anatomy
front: Plane (gliding) joints — examples?
back: Intercarpal, intertarsal, acromioclavicular, costotransverse, costovertebral, and sternocostal (except the 1st).

subject: Anatomy
front: Parallel and fusiform muscles — examples?
back: Parallel: thyrohyoid, sternohyoid, rectus abdominis; fusiform: biceps brachii, digastric.

subject: Anatomy
front: Triangular muscles — examples?
back: Temporalis, adductor longus, trapezius.

subject: Anatomy
front: Spiral/twisted muscles — examples?
back: Pectoralis major, latissimus dorsi, trapezius.

subject: Anatomy
front: Cruciate muscles — examples?
back: Sternocleidomastoid, adductor magnus.

subject: Anatomy
front: Unipennate, bipennate and multipennate muscles — examples?
back: Unipennate: flexor pollicis longus, extensor digitorum longus; bipennate: rectus femoris, flexor hallucis longus; multipennate: tibialis anterior, subscapularis, deltoid.

subject: Anatomy
front: Digastric — nerve supply?
back: Anterior belly – nerve to mylohyoid (branch of V3); posterior belly – facial nerve.

subject: Anatomy
front: Flexor digitorum profundus — nerve supply?
back: Medial half – ulnar nerve; lateral half – anterior interosseous (branch of median nerve).

subject: Anatomy
front: Adductor magnus — nerve supply?
back: Adductor part – obturator nerve; hamstring part – tibial nerve.

subject: Anatomy
front: Biceps femoris — nerve supply?
back: Long head – tibial nerve; short head – common peroneal nerve.

subject: Anatomy
front: Pectineus — nerve supply?
back: Anterior fibres – femoral nerve; posterior fibres – obturator nerve.

subject: Anatomy
front: Brachialis — nerve supply?
back: Proprioceptive – radial nerve; motor – musculocutaneous nerve.

subject: Anatomy
front: Flexor pollicis brevis — nerve supply?
back: Superficial head – median nerve; deep head – deep branch of ulnar nerve.

subject: Anatomy
front: Pectoralis major & minor — nerve supply?
back: Medial and lateral pectoral nerves.

subject: Anatomy
front: Subscapularis — nerve supply?
back: Upper and lower subscapular nerves.

subject: Anatomy
front: Reticular fibres are absent in?
back: The thymus.

subject: Anatomy
front: Ligamentum flavum is rich in?
back: Elastin.

subject: Anatomy
front: Dense irregular collagen is found in?
back: The dermis.

subject: Anatomy
front: Which leg compartment lacks a neurovascular bundle?
back: The superficial posterior compartment of the leg.

subject: Anatomy
front: Where does the ansa cervicalis lie relative to the carotid sheath?
back: In the anterior part of (on) the carotid sheath.

subject: Anatomy
front: The common carotid artery divides at what level?
back: The upper border of the thyroid cartilage (C4).

subject: Anatomy
front: The transverse facial artery is a branch of?
back: The superficial temporal artery.

subject: Anatomy
front: Structures crossed by the root of the mesentery?
back: 3rd part of duodenum, aorta, IVC, right gonadal vessels, right ureter, genitofemoral nerve, and right psoas.

subject: Anatomy
front: Normal prostate volume?
back: 20 mL.

subject: Anatomy
front: What is special about the clavicle's ossification?
back: It has 2 primary ossification centres (exception to the single-centre rule), is the only long bone with membranous (intramembranous) ossification, and is the first bone to start ossifying (5th week).

subject: Anatomy
front: Which structures are ~25 cm long?
back: Oesophagus, duodenum, ureter, descending colon.

subject: Anatomy
front: Which structures are ~45 cm long?
back: Spinal cord, thoracic duct, vas deferens, sartorius, femur, transverse colon.

subject: Anatomy
front: Relation of the ureter to the gonadal vessels?
back: The ureter passes posterior to the gonadal vessels.

subject: Anatomy
front: Relation of the ureter at the pelvic brim?
back: It crosses anterior to the bifurcation of the common iliac artery.

subject: Anatomy
front: Relation of the ureter to the uterine artery?
back: The ureter passes posterior (inferior) to the uterine artery ('water under the bridge').

subject: Anatomy
front: Trunk dermatome landmarks: apex of axilla, nipple, xiphoid, umbilicus, inguinal region?
back: T2 – apex of axilla; T4 – nipple; T6 – xiphoid; T10 – umbilicus; T12 – inguinal region.

subject: Anatomy
front: Upper limb reflex root values: biceps, brachioradialis (supinator), triceps?
back: Biceps C5–6; brachioradialis C5–6; triceps C6–7–8.

subject: Anatomy
front: Branches of the lumbar plexus on the posterior abdominal wall?
back: Subcostal (T12), iliohypogastric, ilioinguinal, genitofemoral, lateral cutaneous nerve of thigh, femoral and obturator nerves.

subject: Anatomy
front: Branches of the external carotid artery?
back: Superior thyroid, Ascending pharyngeal, Lingual, Facial, Occipital, Posterior auricular, Maxillary, Superficial temporal (ascending pharyngeal is the only medial branch).

subject: Anatomy
