front: Largest branch and the terminal branches of the ECA?
back: Maxillary is the largest branch; the two terminal branches are the maxillary and superficial temporal arteries.

subject: Anatomy
front: Maxillary artery 1st part branches (mnemonic DAMIA)?
back: Deep auricular, Anterior tympanic, Middle meningeal, Inferior alveolar, Accessory meningeal arteries.

subject: Anatomy
front: Maxillary artery 2nd part branches?
back: Masseteric, deep temporal, pterygoid and buccal arteries.

subject: Anatomy
front: Maxillary artery 3rd part branches?
back: Posterior superior alveolar, infraorbital, greater & lesser palatine, artery of the pterygoid canal, sphenopalatine (terminal) and pharyngeal.

subject: Anatomy
front: Main branches of the mandibular nerve (V3)?
back: Anterior division (mainly motor) – masseteric, deep temporal, nerve to lateral pterygoid, buccal (sensory); posterior division (mainly sensory) – auriculotemporal, lingual, inferior alveolar (→ mental & incisive; gives nerve to mylohyoid); from the main trunk – nerve to medial pterygoid, tensor tympani and tensor veli palatini.

subject: Anatomy
front: Lockjaw is due to spasm of which muscle, and lockjaw on yawning?
back: Lockjaw (trismus) – medial pterygoid; lockjaw while yawning – lateral pterygoid.

subject: Anatomy
front: Which structures pierce the buccinator?
back: The buccal nerve (V3), buccal vessels and the parotid duct.

subject: Anatomy
front: The investing (superficial) layer of deep cervical fascia encloses which structures?
back: Masseter, parotid gland and submandibular gland (and SCM & trapezius).

subject: Anatomy
front: Route by which facial infection spreads to the cavernous sinus (danger area of face)?
back: Facial vein → deep facial vein → pterygoid venous plexus → emissary vein → cavernous sinus.

subject: Anatomy
front: Contents of the carotid sheath and their arrangement?
back: Common/internal carotid artery (medial), internal jugular vein (lateral), and the vagus nerve (posterior, between them); the sympathetic trunk lies behind/outside the sheath.

subject: Anatomy
front: Cutaneous (sensory) branches of the cervical plexus (mnemonic LATS)?
back: Lesser occipital, greater Auricular, Transverse cervical and Supraclavicular nerves.

subject: Anatomy
front: What is Erb's point (nerve point of the neck)?
back: The point at the posterior border of SCM where the cutaneous branches of the cervical plexus emerge (~C2-C3); a block there anaesthetises the neck.

subject: Anatomy
front: Ansa cervicalis — root values and what it supplies?
back: Superior root C1 (travels with the hypoglossal nerve) + inferior root C2-C3; supplies the infrahyoid (strap) muscles – sternohyoid, sternothyroid and omohyoid.

subject: Anatomy
front: Which strap muscles are NOT supplied by the ansa cervicalis?
back: Thyrohyoid and geniohyoid (by C1 fibres carried directly on the hypoglossal nerve).

subject: Anatomy
front: Boundaries and subdivisions of the anterior triangle of the neck?
back: Bounded by the midline, mandible and anterior border of SCM; subdivided into submandibular (digastric), submental, muscular and carotid triangles.

subject: Anatomy
front: Boundaries and contents of the posterior triangle of the neck?
back: Bounded by SCM, trapezius and the clavicle; contains the accessory nerve (XI), brachial plexus roots/trunks, subclavian artery and cervical plexus branches; divided by omohyoid into occipital and supraclavicular triangles.

subject: Anatomy
front: Submandibular (digastric) triangle — boundaries and contents?
back: Bounded by the two bellies of digastric and the mandible; contains the submandibular gland, lymph nodes and the hypoglossal nerve.

subject: Anatomy
front: The accessory nerve (XI) supplies which muscles, and injury causes?
back: SCM and trapezius; injury → trapezius weakness (shoulder droop).

subject: Anatomy
front: Sertoli cell functions/products?
back: MIF (Müllerian inhibiting factor), inhibin B, androgen-binding protein, and forming the blood-testis barrier.

subject: Anatomy
front: Duration of spermatogenesis, total maturation and fertilisable span?
back: Spermatogenesis ~72 days + epididymal maturation ~14 days = ~90 days; fertilisable span ~72 hours.

subject: Anatomy
front: At what stage and until when is meiosis I arrested in oogenesis?
back: Prophase I (diplotene stage), from before birth until ovulation.

subject: Anatomy
front: At what stage and until when is meiosis II arrested in oogenesis?
back: Metaphase II, until fertilization (sperm entry).

subject: Anatomy
front: What does the primary oocyte produce at ovulation?
back: It completes meiosis I → a secondary oocyte + the first polar body (the secondary oocyte then arrests in metaphase II).

subject: Anatomy
front: What is capacitation?
back: Sperm acquiring motility/fertilizing ability in the female reproductive tract (via Ca2+ & HCO3- influx).

subject: Anatomy
front: What is the acrosome reaction?
back: Release of hyaluronidase to digest the corona radiata.

subject: Anatomy
front: Cortical reaction vs zona reaction?
back: Cortical reaction – cortical granules released; zona reaction – zona pellucida hardening (ZP2/ZP3) that prevents polyspermy.

subject: Anatomy
front: Early development timeline (fertilization → gastrulation)?
back: Fertilization D0; cleavage D1-4; compaction D3-4; blastocyst/cavitation D5; implantation D6-10; bilaminar disc 2nd week; gastrulation 3rd week.

subject: Anatomy
front: Three steps of implantation and their molecules?
back: Apposition (L-selectin), Adhesion (integrins), Invasion (MMPs).

subject: Anatomy
front: All three germ layers arise from which layer, and in what order?
back: From the epiblast; order endoderm → mesoderm → ectoderm.

subject: Anatomy
front: What determines the craniocaudal axis of the embryo?
back: HOX genes (with Brachyury).

subject: Anatomy
front: What is the most common congenital tumour?
back: Sacrococcygeal (sacral) teratoma.

subject: Anatomy
front: The intra-embryonic coelom forms which body cavities?
back: The pleural, peritoneal and pericardial cavities.

subject: Anatomy
front: Gastrulating embryo structures from cranial to caudal?
back: Prochordal plate (cranial), primitive knot (Hensen's node), primitive streak, cloacal membrane (caudal).

subject: Anatomy
front: What forms the bifurcate ligament of the foot?
back: The calcaneocuboid and calcaneonavicular ligaments.

subject: Anatomy
front: Rupture of which ligament causes flat foot (pes planus)?
back: The spring (plantar calcaneonavicular) ligament.

subject: Anatomy
front: What is seen at the transpyloric plane (L1)?
back: Fundus of the gallbladder, origin of the SMA, and the 1st & 4th parts of the duodenum (also the pylorus and hila of the kidneys).

subject: Anatomy
front: What lies at the T4/T5 (sternal angle) plane?
back: T4 – aortic arch with trachea; T5 – SVC, ascending aorta and main pulmonary trunk (± azygos vein).

subject: Anatomy
front: At which level are the suprarenal (adrenal) glands seen on axial section?

subject: Anatomy
front: At which levels does the IVC appear bilobed and then bifurcate/form?
back: IVC appears bilobed at L4 and bifurcates (is formed) at L5.
